_meta.title: Loan recovery glossary
_meta.source: Standard Indian banking / NBFC / microfinance recovery terminology; RBI and MFIN usage
_meta.summary: Operational definitions of recovery terms, with why each matters to a practitioner and related terms for retrieval.
_meta.note: Definitions are general domain knowledge. Exact regulatory thresholds referenced here (e.g. SARFAESI/DRT amounts, NPA day-count) are summarized in legal/ and rbi/ and carry TODO: verify where not confirmed against the primary source.

DPD.definition: Days Past Due — the number of days a payment (EMI/instalment) has remained unpaid past its due date.
DPD.importance: The single most-used severity signal. Drives bucketing, provisioning, NPA classification, and which recovery action is appropriate.

NPA.definition: Non-Performing Asset — a loan on which principal or interest is overdue for 90 days or more (standard RBI norm for banks; see rbi/ for NBFC/MFI specifics).
NPA.importance: Marks the point where a loan is recognized as impaired, triggers provisioning and usually escalates recovery from soft to formal/legal.

PAR30.definition: Portfolio at Risk over 30 days — the outstanding principal of all loans with any instalment overdue more than 30 days, as a percentage of the total portfolio.
PAR30.importance: Early-stress indicator widely used in microfinance to gauge book health before loans hit NPA.

PAR60.definition: Portfolio at Risk over 60 days — outstanding of loans overdue more than 60 days as a percentage of the portfolio.
PAR60.importance: Mid-stage stress indicator; loans here are close to NPA and warrant formal action.

PAR90.definition: Portfolio at Risk over 90 days — outstanding of loans overdue more than 90 days as a percentage of the portfolio; closely tracks NPA.
PAR90.importance: Loans here are effectively non-performing; signals the legal/settlement stage.

Lok Adalat.definition: A statutory alternative-dispute-resolution forum under the Legal Services Authorities Act, 1987, that settles disputes by compromise; its award is final, binding, and has the status of a civil court decree with no appeal.
Lok Adalat.importance: The primary realistic forum for small-ticket recovery — cheap, fast, consent-based, and court-fee-refunding. Most sub-₹1 lakh cases route here.

Permanent Lok Adalat.definition: A Lok Adalat constituted under Section 22-B of the Legal Services Authorities Act, 1987 for public-utility services, which can adjudicate (decide on merits) up to a pecuniary limit even if one party does not consent to settlement.
Permanent Lok Adalat.importance: Differs from an ordinary Lok Adalat in that it can decide, not only conciliate; relevant where the dispute concerns a notified public-utility service. TODO: verify current pecuniary limit against the primary source.

SARFAESI.definition: The Securitisation and Reconstruction of Financial Assets and Enforcement of Security Interest Act, 2002 — lets a secured creditor enforce security (seize/sell) without court intervention after a Section 13(2) demand notice.
SARFAESI.importance: Powerful but usually UNAVAILABLE for sub-₹1 lakh unsecured recovery: it needs a secured asset and meets a minimum loan size, and applies only to notified lenders. Knowing it does NOT apply narrows the realistic options. TODO: verify current minimum loan amount and NBFC applicability thresholds.

DRT.definition: Debts Recovery Tribunal — a tribunal under the Recovery of Debts and Bankruptcy Act, 1993 (formerly RDDBFI Act) for recovery of debts by banks and financial institutions above a pecuniary threshold.
DRT.importance: Generally UNAVAILABLE for small-ticket recovery because of its high minimum claim amount; sub-₹1 lakh cases cannot use it. TODO: verify current minimum (commonly cited as ₹20 lakh).

ARC.definition: Asset Reconstruction Company — an RBI-registered entity that buys non-performing assets from lenders (often at a discount) and works to recover them.
ARC.importance: Relevant when a portfolio of bad loans is sold; individual small-ticket cases are usually recovered in-house or via agencies rather than sold one-off.

Recovery Agent.definition: A third-party individual or agency engaged by a lender to pursue collections, subject to RBI conduct guidelines on communication, identification, and conduct.
Recovery Agent.importance: Most field collection is done by agents; their conduct is heavily regulated, and violations create legal and reputational liability for the lender. See rbi/recovery-agents.md.

Recovery Practitioner.definition: An experienced professional (often an advocate or senior collections specialist) who manages and executes the legal-and-operational recovery of defaulted loans.
Recovery Practitioner.importance: Undark's primary user. Knows the law and process; needs the case reconstructed, not explained.

Provisioning.definition: Capital a lender sets aside against expected losses on loans, scaled by how overdue/impaired the loan is.
Provisioning.importance: Explains lender urgency: higher provisioning eats into capital, so lenders push to recover or settle before/after NPA classification.

Write-off.definition: Removing a loan from the active books as a loss (often after full provisioning); the debt may still be legally recoverable.
Write-off.importance: A written-off loan is still collectable — recovery on written-off accounts is often pure upside for the lender, changing settlement appetite.

Settlement.definition: A negotiated closure where the borrower pays an agreed (usually reduced) amount to fully discharge the account; commonly a One-Time Settlement (OTS).
Settlement.importance: The realistic end-state for most small-ticket recovery; the practitioner's skill is setting a fair, recoverable figure.

Charge-off.definition: Accounting recognition that a debt is unlikely to be collected and is moved off active receivables as a loss; similar in effect to write-off.
Charge-off.importance: Like write-off, signals the lender has booked the loss — recovery is upside and settlement flexibility rises.

Demand Notice.definition: A formal written demand to the borrower to pay the outstanding amount, typically with a deadline.
Demand Notice.importance: Often the first formal escalation and a prerequisite/evidence step before legal action; under SARFAESI specifically it is the Section 13(2) notice.

Pre-litigation Notice.definition: A notice sent before filing a case (including before a Lok Adalat reference), giving the borrower a final opportunity to pay or settle.
Pre-litigation Notice.importance: Builds the evidence trail of good-faith effort and is the standard precursor to Lok Adalat or civil action in small-ticket recovery.

Civil Suit.definition: A money-recovery suit filed in a civil court to obtain a decree for the outstanding debt.
Civil Suit.importance: The fallback when settlement/Lok Adalat fails and faster tracks (SARFAESI/DRT) are unavailable; slower and costlier, so used selectively for small tickets.

Order 37.definition: Order XXXVII of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 — a summary-suit procedure for recovery on negotiable instruments and written contracts for a liquidated debt, where the defendant must seek leave to defend.
Order 37.importance: A faster civil route than an ordinary suit when the debt rests on a cheque, promissory note, or clear written contract — common in recovery.

NI Act Section 138.definition: Section 138 of the Negotiable Instruments Act, 1881 — criminalizes dishonour of a cheque for insufficiency of funds, subject to strict statutory notice and filing timelines.
NI Act Section 138.importance: A strong lever when the borrower gave a cheque that bounced; the timelines are unforgiving, so the practitioner must act fast. See legal/ni-act.md.

Secured Loan.definition: A loan backed by collateral (property, vehicle, gold) the lender can enforce against on default.
Secured Loan.importance: Security unlocks faster enforcement (e.g. SARFAESI for eligible secured creditors); its presence/absence changes the entire recovery strategy.

Unsecured Loan.definition: A loan with no collateral; recovery depends on notices, settlement, and money suits rather than enforcing security.
Unsecured Loan.importance: Most small-ticket and microfinance loans are unsecured — meaning SARFAESI is out and Lok Adalat / NI Act / civil suit are the levers.

MFI.definition: Microfinance Institution — a lender providing very small, typically unsecured loans to low-income borrowers, often in groups.
MFI.importance: Defines the core small-ticket segment Undark serves; MFI loans are unsecured, high-volume, and overwhelmingly resolved via settlement/Lok Adalat.

NBFC.definition: Non-Banking Financial Company — an RBI-regulated lender that is not a bank, including many consumer and microfinance lenders.
NBFC.importance: Most small-ticket lenders are NBFCs; their applicable RBI rules (fair practices, recovery agents, digital lending) govern what recovery conduct is allowed.

AUM.definition: Assets Under Management — the total value of loans/assets a lender or institution manages.
AUM.importance: Context for portfolio scale; not a per-case signal but useful for understanding a lender's stakes.

GNPA.definition: Gross Non-Performing Assets — total NPAs before deducting provisions, usually expressed as a ratio of gross advances.
GNPA.importance: Headline measure of book stress; rising GNPA in a segment signals more recovery volume and tighter lender settlement appetite.

Net NPA.definition: Gross NPAs minus provisions held against them, as a ratio of net advances.
Net NPA.importance: Shows the unprovided portion of bad loans — the residual loss the lender still carries and wants to recover.

Right Party Contact.definition: Right Party Contact (RPC) — actually reaching the correct borrower (not a relative or wrong number) during a collection attempt.
Right Party Contact.importance: A core collections efficiency metric; without RPC there is no recovery conversation. Also bounded by RBI rules against contacting third parties.

Field Visit.definition: An in-person visit to the borrower's home or business by a recovery agent/officer.
Field Visit.importance: A real-world collection step (out of Undark's V0 scope) heavily constrained by RBI conduct rules; relevant as context, not as something Undark recommends.

Bucket.definition: A grouping of accounts by delinquency stage, usually by DPD range (e.g. 0–30, 31–60, 61–90, 90+).
Bucket.importance: Determines collection strategy and team handling; the bucket a case sits in shapes whether it is soft-collections or legal/settlement.

DPD Bucket.definition: A specific DPD range used to bucket an account (e.g. X-bucket = 1–30 DPD, etc.).
DPD Bucket.importance: Operational shorthand for severity; aligns recovery action to delinquency stage.

Recovery Rate.definition: The proportion of an outstanding amount (or portfolio) actually recovered, usually as a percentage.
Recovery Rate.importance: Measures effectiveness of a channel or strategy; low recovery rates via formal channels are why settlement/Lok Adalat dominate small tickets.

Promise to Pay.definition: Promise to Pay (PTP) — a borrower's commitment to pay a stated amount by a stated date, captured during a collection contact.
Promise to Pay.importance: Tracks intent and is a soft-collections milestone; broken PTPs justify escalation to formal/legal steps.

Skip Trace.definition: The process of locating a borrower who has become unreachable or absconded, using available data and field inquiry.
Skip Trace.importance: Precondition to any recovery on an absconding borrower; also bounded by privacy and RBI third-party-contact rules.

Recovery Commission.definition: The fee a recovery agent/practitioner earns, usually a percentage of the amount recovered.
Recovery Commission.importance: Aligns practitioner incentives with throughput and recovery size; explains why faster case turnaround directly increases earnings — Undark's value proposition.
